summary: DHH reflects on Basecamp's recovery one year after the controversial decision to ban political discussions at work, which led to roughly a third of employees departing. He frames the crisis as ultimately beneficial, arguing the company emerged stronger and more aligned after the turbulent period. Writing from a company meetup in Miami, he describes a reinvigorated team with better morale and shared values. He credits the concept of antifragility and argues that the public backlash was far less consequential than feared in the moment.
key_points: Basecamp's post-crisis recovery exceeded expectations, with the company now bigger and on a better trajectory than before the political speech policy controversy
Real crises provide clarity by forcing you to question fundamentals and choose a new direction rather than defaulting to the status quo
The concept of antifragility applies to organizations — bouncing back stronger from breakage is better than being strong but brittle
Most customers and prospective employees care about the product and workplace fit, not your temporary controversies
Basecamp now includes an explicit statement about its no-politics-at-work policy in all job listings, filtering for cultural alignment upfront
money_quotes: When things are difficult, it's tempting to lose hope, regret your principles, and shrivel from the occasion. But rarely is the outlook as dim as you imagine, and rarely are the consequences as harsh as you envision.
The gift of a real crisis is the clarity it brings by forcing you to question the fundamentals.
You can be strong yet brittle. Capable of withstanding immense pressure, but shattering in a thousand pieces if your limits are exceeded. Or you can be antifragile.
The vast majority of customers care about you delivering a great service at a reasonable price above all else.
The vast majority of prospective employees care about whether they can see themselves at your company, not how others couldn't.
The uncertainty of what might happened replaced by the certainty of what did happen. And the relief that not only wasn't it as bad as feared, but it turned out better than we dared hope.
The world is full of great people who not only can but embrace working with others of different political backgrounds without litigating their differences at work.
key_insight: Organizational crises that force you to stand by your principles can ultimately strengthen a company by filtering for cultural alignment and providing clarity of purpose.
